theorems.title: PAC-Bayesian bound for pruned model mixtures
theorems.proofNote: The paper derives Theorem 1 from Lemma 2 immediately after stating that lemma; Lemma 2 is proved later in Section 3.
theorems.statementHtml:
With probability at least \(1-\delta\) over the selection of the sample \(S\) of size \(m\) we have the following for all pruned distributions \(Q\).

\[\ell(Q)\leq \hat\ell(Q,S)+\sqrt{\frac{D(Q\mathbin\|P)+\ln\frac{1}{\delta}+\frac{5}{2}\ln m+8}{2m-1}}\]
theorems.proofHtml:
Theorem 1 is proved from the following lemma.

Lemma 2 is proved in section 3. Note that if \(\ln\frac{1}{\delta}>2m\) or \(D(Q\mathbin\|P)>2m\) then the bound in theorem 1 follows from the fact that \(\ell(Q)\leq 1\). If \(\ln\frac{1}{\delta}\leq 2m\) and \(D(Q\mathbin\|P)\leq 2m\) then theorem 1 follows from lemma 2 by an application of Jensen’s inequality. Assuming that we are only interested in cases where the premises of lemma 2 hold, lemma 2 gives a tighter bound than theorem 1.

theorems.title: Pruned-distribution deviation bound
theorems.section: 2 Statement of Main Results; 3 Proof of Lemma 2
theorems.proofNote: Section 3 proves the lemma through Lemmas 3–7. Their statements and proofs are separate entries; the encompassing argument and the final calculation are transcribed here.
theorems.statementHtml:
For \(\ln\frac{1}{\delta}\leq 2m\) we have that with probability \(1-\delta\) over the selection of a sample \(S\) of size \(m\) the following holds for all pruned distributions satisfying \(D(Q\mathbin\|P)\leq 2m\).

\[\ell(Q)\leq \hat\ell(Q,S)+\sum_i Q_i\sqrt{\frac{\ln\frac{Q_i}{P_i}+\ln\frac{1}{\delta}+\frac{5}{2}\ln m+8}{2m-1}}\]
theorems.proofHtml:
The departure point for the proof of lemma 2 is the following where \(S\) is a sample of size \(m\) and \(\gamma_i\) abbreviates \(|\ell(c_i)-\hat\ell(c_i,S)|\).

To prove lemma 2 we consider selecting a sample \(S\). Lemma 3 implies that with probability at least \(1-\delta\) we have the following.

\[\sum_i P_i e^{(2m-1)\gamma_i^2}\leq\frac{4m}{\delta}. \tag{5}\]

After selecting \(S\) satisfying (5) we consider an arbitrary “posterior” distribution \(Q\). Lemma 2 can be viewed as an upper bound on the quantity \(\ell(Q)-\hat\ell(Q,S)\). But note that

\[\ell(Q)-\hat\ell(Q,S)=\sum_iQ_i\bigl(\ell(c_i)-\hat\ell(c_i,S)\bigr)\leq\sum_iQ_i\gamma_i.\]

So to prove lemma 2 it now suffices to show that (5) plus \(\ln\frac{1}{\delta}\leq 2m\) implies the following for all pruned distributions \(Q\) such that \(D(Q\mathbin\|P)\leq 2m\).

\[\sum_iQ_i\gamma_i\leq\sum_iQ_i\sqrt{\frac{\ln\frac{Q_i}{P_i}+\ln\frac{1}{\delta}+\frac{5}{2}\ln m+8}{2m-1}}. \tag{6}\]

To prove (6) for a given \(Q\) we select \(\gamma_i\) so as to maximize the quantity \(\sum_iQ_i\gamma_i\) subject to the constraint (5). Using Lagrange multipliers we set the gradient of the constraint to be equal to a multiplier \(\lambda\) times the gradient of the objective function.

\[P_i2(2m-1)\gamma_i e^{(2m-1)\gamma_i^2}=\lambda Q_i.\]

Formula (6) is trivially true if \(Q_i>0\) but \(P_i=0\) for some \(i\). So we can assume without loss of generality that \(P_i>0\) whenever \(Q_i>0\). This allows the above to be rewritten as follows.

\[2(2m-1)\gamma_i e^{(2m-1)\gamma_i^2}=\frac{\lambda Q_i}{P_i}.\]

Note that \(2(2m-1)xe^{(2m-1)x^2}\) is an unbounded monotonically increasing function of \(x\). We now define \(\Delta_i(\lambda)\) to be the unique non-negative value satisfying the following.

\[2(2m-1)\Delta_i(\lambda)e^{(2m-1)\Delta_i^2(\lambda)}=\frac{\lambda Q_i}{P_i}. \tag{7}\]

Now note that \(\sum_iP_i e^{(2m-1)\Delta_i^2(\lambda)}\) is an unbounded monotonically increasing function of \(\lambda\). We now define \(\lambda^*\) to be the unique nonnegative value such that we have the following.

\[\sum_iP_i e^{(2m-1)\Delta_i^2(\lambda^*)}=\frac{4m}{\delta}. \tag{8}\]

Note that \(\Delta_i(0)=0\) and \(\sum_iP_i e^{(2m-1)\Delta_i^2(0)}=1<\frac{4m}{\delta}\). So we must have \(\lambda^*>0\) and hence \(\Delta_i(\lambda^*)>0\) for \(Q_i>0\).

It now suffices to bound \(\sum_iQ_i\Delta_i(\lambda^*)\). Equation (7) implies that for \(\lambda\gg 1\) and \(Q_i\geq P_i\) we have the following.

\[\Delta_i(\lambda)\approx\sqrt{\frac{\ln\frac{\lambda Q_i}{P_i}}{2m-1}}.\]

This approximate relationship is made more precise in the following two lemmas.

Lemma 2 now follows from the following calculation.

\[\begin{aligned}\ell(Q)-\hat\ell(Q,S)&\leq\sum_iQ_i\gamma_i\\&\leq\sum_iQ_i\Delta_i(\lambda^*)\\&\leq\sum_iQ_i\Delta_i\!\left(\frac{64e^2m^{5/2}}{\delta}\right)\\&\leq\sum_iQ_i\sqrt{\frac{\ln\frac{64e^2m^{5/2}Q_i}{\delta P_i}}{2m-1}}\\&\leq\sum_iQ_i\sqrt{\frac{\ln\frac{Q_i}{P_i}+\frac{5}{2}\ln m+\ln\frac{1}{\delta}+8}{2m-1}}.\end{aligned}\]

theorems.title: Exponential deviation sum
theorems.proofHtml:
It suffices to prove the following.

\[E_S E_{c_i}\,e^{(2m-1)\gamma_i^2}\leq 4m. \tag{3}\]

Lemma 3 follows from (3) by an application of Markov’s inequality. To prove (3) it suffices to prove the following for any individual given concept.

\[E_S e^{(2m-1)\gamma^2}\leq 4m. \tag{4}\]

By the Chernoff bound we have \(P(\gamma\geq x)\leq 2e^{-2mx^2}\). We now consider the density function \(f(\gamma)\) maximizing \(\int_0^\infty e^{(2m-1)\gamma^2}f(\gamma)\,d\gamma\) subject to the constraint that \(\int_x^\infty f(\gamma)\,d\gamma\leq 2e^{-2mx^2}\). The maximum occurs when we have \(\int_x^\infty f(\gamma)\,d\gamma=2e^{-2mx^2}\) which is realized when \(f(\gamma)=8m\gamma e^{-2m\gamma^2}\). So we have the following.

\[\begin{aligned}E_S e^{(2m-1)\gamma^2}&\leq\int_0^\infty e^{(2m-1)\gamma^2}f(\gamma)\,d\gamma\\&=\int_0^\infty 8m\gamma e^{(2m-1)\gamma^2}e^{-2m\gamma^2}\,d\gamma\\&=\int_0^\infty 8m\gamma e^{-\gamma^2}\,d\gamma\\&=4m.\end{aligned}\]

theorems.title: Lagrange optimum for the deviation sum
theorems.proofNote: The displayed comparison in the supplied PDF prints \(\gamma_i\) with the first fraction and \(\gamma_j\) with the second, while the exponential subscripts are \(j\) and \(k\). Those printed subscripts are preserved rather than silently repaired.
theorems.proofHtml:
If \(\gamma_i<0\) then replacing \(\gamma_i\) by \(-\gamma_i\) increases \(\sum_iQ_i\gamma_i\) without violating (5). Hence we can assume without loss of generality that \(\gamma_i\geq 0\). Also, note that if \(\sum_iP_i e^{(2m-1)\gamma_i^2}<\frac{4m}{\delta}\) then \(\sum_iQ_i\gamma_i\) can be increased by increasing some \(\gamma_i\). Hence we can assume without loss of generality that \(\sum_iP_i e^{(2m-1)\gamma_i^2}=\frac{4m}{\delta}\). Also, if \(\gamma_j>0\) for some \(j\) such that \(Q_j=0\) then \(\sum_iQ_i\gamma_i\) can be increased by setting \(\gamma_j=0\) while increasing some other \(\gamma_k\) with \(Q_k>0\). Hence we can assume without loss of generality that \(\gamma_j=0\) whenever \(Q_j=0\). Finally, if there exist two indices \(j\) and \(k\) with \(Q_j>0\) and \(Q_k>0\) and

\[\frac{P_j}{Q_j}\gamma_i e^{(2m-1)\gamma_j^2}>\frac{P_k}{Q_k}\gamma_j e^{(2m-1)\gamma_k^2}\]

then \(\sum_iQ_i\gamma_i\) can be increased by increasing \(\gamma_k\) and decreasing \(\gamma_j\) while holding \(\sum_iP_i e^{(2m-1)\gamma_i^2}\) constant. So we can assume without loss of generality that there exists a value \(\lambda'\) such that for all indices \(i\) with \(Q_i>0\) we have

\[2(2m-1)\gamma_i e^{(2m-1)\gamma_i^2}=\frac{\lambda'Q_i}{P_i}.\]

This implies that \(\gamma_i=\Delta_i(\lambda')\). But we then have that \(\sum_iP_i e^{(2m-1)\Delta_i^2(\lambda')}=\frac{4m}{\delta}\) which implies that \(\lambda'=\lambda^*\). So we now have that \(\gamma_i=\Delta_i(\lambda^*)\) which implies the result.

theorems.title: Upper bound on the Lagrange solution
theorems.statementHtml:
For \(m\geq 1\), \(P_i>0\), \(Q_i\geq P_i\), and \(\lambda\geq e\), we have the following.

\[\Delta_i(\lambda)\leq\sqrt{\frac{\ln\frac{\lambda Q_i}{P_i}}{2m-1}}.\]
theorems.proofHtml:
Let \(f(x)\) be the function mapping \(x\) to the quantity \(2(2m-1)xe^{(2m-1)x^2}\). By definition, \(\Delta_i(\lambda)\) satisfies \(f(\Delta_i(\lambda))=\frac{\lambda Q_i}{P_i}\). Note that for \(x\geq 0\) we have that \(f(x)\) is a monotonically increasing function. Hence for \(x\geq 0\) and \(f(x)\geq\frac{\lambda Q_i}{P_i}\) we must have \(\Delta_i(\lambda)\leq x\). Under the assumptions of the lemma we have \(\ln\frac{\lambda Q_i}{P_i}\geq 1\) which implies the following.

\[\begin{aligned}f\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{\ln\frac{\lambda Q_i}{P_i}}{2m-1}}\right)&=2\sqrt{(2m-1)\ln\frac{\lambda Q_i}{P_i}}\,\frac{\lambda Q_i}{P_i}\\&\geq\frac{\lambda Q_i}{P_i}.\end{aligned}\]

theorems.title: Lower bound on the Lagrange solution
theorems.statementHtml:
For \(m\geq 1\), \(P_i>0\), \(Q_i\geq P_i\), and \(\lambda\geq e\) we have the following.

\[\Delta_i(\lambda)\geq\sqrt{\frac{\ln\frac{\lambda Q_i}{P_i}-\frac{1}{2}\ln m-\ln\ln\frac{\lambda Q_i}{P_i}-2}{2m-1}}.\]
theorems.proofHtml:
By an argument similar to that in the proof of lemma 5, to show that \(\Delta_i(\lambda)\geq x\) it suffices to show that \(f(x)\leq\frac{\lambda Q_i}{P_i}\). In particular we have the following.

\[\begin{aligned}&f\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{\ln\frac{\lambda Q_i}{P_i}-\frac{1}{2}\ln m-\ln\ln\frac{\lambda Q_i}{P_i}-2}{2m-1}}\right)\\&=2\sqrt{(2m-1)\!\left(\ln\frac{\lambda Q_i}{P_i}-\frac{1}{2}\ln m-\ln\ln\frac{\lambda Q_i}{P_i}-2\right)}\,\frac{\lambda Q_i}{P_i}\frac{1}{\sqrt m\ln\frac{\lambda Q_i}{P_i}e^2}\\&\leq4\sqrt{m\ln\frac{\lambda Q_i}{P_i}}\,\frac{\lambda Q_i}{P_i}\frac{1}{\sqrt m\ln\frac{\lambda Q_i}{P_i}e^2}\\&=\frac{\lambda Q_i}{P_i}\frac{1}{\sqrt{\ln\frac{\lambda Q_i}{P_i}}}\frac{4}{e^2}\\&\leq\frac{\lambda Q_i}{P_i}.\end{aligned}\]

theorems.title: Upper bound on the Lagrange multiplier
theorems.proofNote: The printed proof says that \(g(x)\geq 2m/\delta\) implies \(\lambda^*\leq x\), although it defines \(g(\lambda^*)=4m/\delta\) and later proves the latter threshold. The printed text is preserved here.
theorems.proofHtml:
Let \(g(\lambda)\) be the function mapping \(\lambda\) to the quantity \(\sum_iP_i e^{(2m-1)\Delta_i^2(\lambda)}\). The quantity \(\lambda^*\) is defined by \(g(\lambda^*)=\frac{4m}{\delta}\). Since \(g\) is a monotonically increasing function, \(g(x)\geq\frac{2m}{\delta}\) implies \(\lambda^*\leq x\). Lemma 6 implies that for \(x\geq e\) we have the following.

\[\begin{aligned}g(x)&=\sum_iP_i e^{(2m-1)\Delta_i^2(x)}\\&\geq\sum_iP_i\frac{xQ_i}{P_i}\frac{1}{\sqrt{m\ln\frac{xQ_i}{P_i}}e^2}\\&=\frac{x}{e^2\sqrt m}\sum_i\frac{Q_i}{\sqrt{\ln\frac{xQ_i}{P_i}}}\\&\geq\frac{x}{e^2\sqrt m}\frac{1}{\sqrt{\sum_iQ_i\ln\frac{xQ_i}{P_i}}}\\&=\frac{x}{e^2\sqrt{m\bigl(D(Q\mathbin\|P)+\ln x\bigr)}}\\&\geq\frac{x}{e^2\sqrt{m(2m+\ln x)}}.\end{aligned}\]

Now inserting \(x=\frac{64e^2m^{5/2}}{\delta}\) we get the following.

\[\begin{aligned}g\!\left(\frac{64e^2m^{5/2}}{\delta}\right)&\geq\frac{64m^2}{\delta\left(2m+\frac{5}{2}\ln m+\ln\frac{1}{\delta}+8\right)}\\&\geq\frac{64m^2}{\delta\left(2m+\frac{5}{2}m+2m+8m\right)}\\&\geq\frac{4m}{\delta}.\end{aligned}\]
